blocks.content: # [3. Bulk Modifications & Refactoring]
Safe Refactoring and Dry-Run Verifications

In this module, you will learn how to direct the agent to perform modifications across multiple files in your workspace. We will discuss safety habits such as running dry-runs, inspecting generated diff outputs, and maintaining a clean git repository for rollback capabilities.
Executing Dry-Runs for Code Changes

When you ask the agent to execute changes across a project, it computes the modifications and can display them in standard unified diff format. Using the --dry-run flag executes the task in memory and prints the diffs without writing any changes to your filesystem. This is crucial for verifying that the agent's changes match your guidelines.
# Request a bulk update to code headers, checking the changes in memory first
agy run -p "Scan all Python files in src/ and add standard author tags to the top of each file." --dry-run
A successful dry-run will print a preview diff similar to this:
--- src/utils.py
+++ src/utils.py
+# Author: Tridorian Labs
def process_data(data):
return data.strip()
Staging and Applying Bulk Refactoring Tasks
When you are ready to write changes, execute the command without the dry-run flag. Follow these guidelines to maintain a stable workspace:
- Ensure git status is clean before starting. If the agent makes a mistake, you can revert all modifications with a single command (git checkout . or git reset --hard).
- Review files side-by-side using standard Git differences before committing the modifications to version control.
- Combine bulk refactoring with test suites. Run your test runner immediately after the agent completes to verify that no logical bugs were introduced.
# 1. Check current branch state
git status
# 2. Execute the modifications across the workspace
agy run -p "Refactor the import statements in src/components to use absolute paths."
# 3. Inspect changes and run test suite
git diff
npm test
Check Your Understanding
Answer the following questions to verify your comprehension of code refactoring safety:
- Question 1: Why should you run --dry-run before applying bulk modifications to a large workspace?
- A) Dry-run speeds up the final execution by caching the files in the cloud.
- B) It allows you to inspect the unified diffs of all proposed file mutations in your terminal to ensure accuracy before writing to disk.
- C) It disables the agent's ability to read your repository file tree.
Feedback: Option B is correct. Dry-run allows you to inspect the changes in unified diff format in the terminal, ensuring that you don't overwrite files with incorrect logic.
- Question 2: What is the recommended strategy to recover if an agentic bulk refactor command introduces a syntax error into your codebase?
- A) Delete the sandbox folder and recreate it.
- B) Run the command again with a prompt asking the agent to fix it.
- C) Revert the unstaged changes using git reset --hard (assuming your workspace was clean before execution).
Feedback: Option C is correct. Keeping a clean git working directory before running changes allows you to instantly recover the codebase state if an agent task fails.
